label: Client Apps
description: Web app (twitter.com/x.com), iOS, and Android native applications.
role: Renders the timeline, handles tweet composition, real-time notifications, and media uploads. Web app uses progressive loading for timeline updates.
whyChosen: React enables rapid iteration on web features. Native mobile apps provide smooth scrolling performance critical for timeline browsing.

cons: Multiple codebases
Feature rollout timing varies across platforms

alternatives.name: PWA
alternatives.context: Progressive web app for unified experience

label: Load Balancer
description: L7 load balancer distributing incoming traffic across the service fleet.
techStack: Custom L7 LB, GCP Load Balancer
role: Distributes millions of requests per second, handles TLS termination, and routes to appropriate service clusters based on request type.
whyChosen: A combination of custom load balancing logic and GCP managed infrastructure provides both flexibility for routing rules and operational simplicity.

pros: Flexible routing rules for different request types
cons: Migration from bare-metal to cloud added complexity
Custom LB logic requires maintenance

alternatives.name: Envoy
alternatives.context: L7 proxy with advanced traffic management

label: API / GraphQL Service
description: GraphQL-based API layer that serves as the unified interface between clients and backend microservices.
role: Provides a single GraphQL endpoint for all client data needs. Aggregates data from multiple backend services (timeline, user, tweet storage) into efficient responses.
whyChosen: GraphQL allows clients to request exactly the data they need, reducing over-fetching. Critical for mobile where bandwidth is limited and latency impacts scroll performance.

cons: Query complexity can impact backend performance
Caching is more complex than REST
N+1 query problem requires careful resolver design

alternatives.name: gRPC
alternatives.context: High-performance binary protocol for internal services

label: Tweet Ingestion Service
description: Handles tweet creation, validation, and persistence with globally unique Snowflake ID assignment.
role: Validates tweet content (length, media, policies), assigns a Snowflake ID for time-ordered uniqueness, persists to the tweet store, and triggers the fanout pipeline.
whyChosen: Snowflake IDs provide time-sortable, globally unique identifiers without coordination — essential for distributed tweet creation across data centers.

pros: Snowflake IDs enable chronological sorting without DB queries
Decoupled from downstream processing via async fanout
cons: Clock synchronization issues can cause ID collisions
Single point of validation can become a bottleneck

alternatives.name: UUIDv7
alternatives.context: Time-ordered UUIDs, no custom generator needed

label: Fanout Service
description: Distributes new tweets to follower timelines using a hybrid push/pull strategy.
techStack: Custom Java/Scala service, Social Graph integration
role: The heart of timeline delivery. For regular users (<10K followers), pushes tweet IDs to follower Redis caches. For celebrities, flags for pull-based merge at read time.
whyChosen: Pure push (fanout-on-write) would overwhelm the system for celebrity accounts. Pure pull would make timeline reads slow. The hybrid approach optimizes both.

pros: Avoids write amplification storms from celebrity tweets
Fast timeline reads for most users
Dynamic threshold tuning based on load
cons: Complex dual-path logic
Celebrity tweet delivery has higher read-time latency
Threshold tuning requires operational monitoring

alternatives.name: Pure fanout-on-write
alternatives.context: Simpler but doesn't scale for high-follower accounts

alternatives.name: Pure fanout-on-read
alternatives.context: Simpler writes but slower reads for everyone

label: Timeline Service
description: Constructs and serves the home timeline by merging precomputed cached timelines with celebrity tweets.
techStack: Scala, Redis client, ML Ranking models
role: Reads the user's precomputed timeline from Redis, merges in celebrity tweets (pull-based), applies ML-based relevance ranking, and returns the ordered feed.
whyChosen: Separating timeline construction from fanout allows independent optimization of read and write paths. ML ranking can be updated without touching the delivery pipeline.

pros: Handles diverse feed compositions (tweets, retweets, ads)
cons: Merge logic adds read-time complexity
Ranking model latency directly impacts user experience

alternatives.name: Pre-ranked timelines
alternatives.context: Rank at write time, simpler reads

alternatives.name: Client-side ranking
alternatives.context: Reduce server load, but less consistent

label: Redis Timeline Cache
description: In-memory cache storing precomputed timelines as sorted sets of tweet IDs for each user.
role: Stores the last ~800 tweet IDs for each user's home timeline. Enables O(1) timeline reads — the single most critical performance path in the entire system.
whyChosen: Redis sorted sets are perfect for timelines — naturally ordered by Snowflake ID (timestamp), with O(log N) insertion and O(1) range reads.

cons: Enormous memory footprint (~100TB+)
Data loss on failure (regeneration from tweet store)
Cost of maintaining hot replicas

label: Tweet Store
description: Persistent storage for all tweets, handling hundreds of billions of records.
role: Durable storage for tweet content, metadata, engagement counts, and media references. Handles massive write throughput during peak events.
whyChosen: Manhattan was Twitter's custom distributed database optimized for their specific access patterns. ScyllaDB migration provides better performance with lower operational overhead.

cons: Custom systems require specialized expertise
Migration to new stores is complex and risky

label: Social Graph Service
description: Manages the follower/following relationships — the core social graph with billions of edges.
role: Stores and queries follow relationships. Answers 'who follows X?' queries needed by the fanout service to determine timeline delivery targets.
whyChosen: The social graph requires specialized adjacency list operations (fan-out queries) at extreme scale. FlockDB was built for exactly this access pattern.

pros: Optimized for adjacency queries at billion-edge scale
cons: Limited query flexibility vs general-purpose graph DB
Sharding complexity for hot users

label: Event Bus (Kafka)
description: Distributed event streaming platform connecting all services through asynchronous event pipelines.
role: Carries tweet creation events to the fanout service, user activity events to analytics, and engagement signals to the trending topics pipeline.
whyChosen: Kafka's durable log provides replay capability essential for rebuilding timelines after failures and feeding multiple consumers from the same event stream.

cons: Operational complexity at scale
Partition ordering constraints
Consumer lag monitoring required
